target.name: (±)-Phaeocaulisin A

structural_features.ring_systems: tetracyclic skeleton embedded with an oxaspirolactone and a fused bicyclic lactone

target.motivation: Phaeocaulisin A is a promising lead compound for developing cancer and autoimmune disease treatment due to its anti-inflammatory and antiproliferation activities. Its unique 8-oxabicyclo[3.2.1]octane core may account for its superior bioactivity.

prior_syntheses.key_strategy: Sharpless dihydroxylation, Heck reaction, and two samarium(II) iodide-mediated reductive cyclizations to form the advanced tetracyclic intermediate

success_criteria.free_text: An efficient total synthesis of (±)-phaeocaulisin A in a concise number of steps, enabled by novel palladium-catalyzed cyclopropanol ring-opening carbonylation, chemo- and stereoselective aldol cyclization, and cascade ketalization-lactonization.

key_disconnections.bond_description: Isomerization of α-methylene γ-butyrolactone to endocyclic α,β-unsaturated γ-butyrolactone
key_disconnections.reasoning: To access the final natural product from an advanced intermediate with a potentially more biologically active α-methylene γ-butyrolactone moiety.
To introduce the exocyclic methylene group, potentially enhancing biological activity.

key_disconnections.bond_description: Formation of the oxa bridge and γ-butyrolactone
key_disconnections.reasoning: To construct the desired tetracyclic skeleton in one step from a C1 tertiary alcohol precursor.
To close the seven-membered ring, a key structural feature of guaianolides.
A novel method to access a key γ-ketoester intermediate, leveraging previously developed methodology for oxaspirolactones and fused bicyclic lactones.
To construct the cyclopropanol precursor for the ring-opening carbonylation.
To introduce the ketone functionality and a hydroxyl group.

key_disconnections.bond_description: Formation of allylated silyl enol ether
key_disconnections.named_strategy: Conjugate silyl addition followed by α-allylation
key_disconnections.reasoning: To quickly assemble the initial cyclopentenone derivative from a commercially available starting material.

retrosynthetic_strategy.rationale: The synthesis aimed to leverage novel palladium-catalyzed cyclopropanol ring-opening carbonylation to access a key γ-ketoester, followed by chemo- and stereoselective aldol cyclization and cascade ketalization-lactonization to construct the complex tetracyclic skeleton of phaeocaulisin A. An initial intramolecular Kulinkovich reaction strategy was abandoned due to synthetic challenges, leading to a revised plan involving an intermolecular Kulinkovich reaction and subsequent carbonylation. The final steps involved introducing an α-methylene γ-butyrolactone moiety and then isomerizing it to the target natural product, also allowing for biological evaluation of the intermediate.

thought.background: The synthesis of phaeocaulisin A requires a complex tetracyclic skeleton. The initial strategy involves building a key intermediate from 3-methyl-2-cyclopentenone.
thought.gap: How to efficiently introduce the allyl group and a silyl group onto the cyclopentenone core.
thought.decision: To introduce the allyl and silyl groups, chose conjugate silyl addition followed by trapping the enolate with allyl bromide, expecting to form 30 in good yield and as a single diastereomer.

outcome.observation: Compound 30 was obtained in 74% yield as a single diastereomer.

evidence.text_span: Our synthesis commenced with 32, which was converted to 30 in 74% yield as a single diastereomer using a conjugate silyl addition followed by trapping the enolate with allyl bromide (Scheme 2A).
evidence.section: Scheme 2. Total Syntheses of (±)-Phaeocaulisin A and Cyclopropanol Ring-Opening Carbonylation to γ-Ketoesters

thought.background: Intermediate 30 has been successfully synthesized. The next step is to introduce a ketone and hydroxyl group.
thought.gap: How to perform a 1,2-addition to introduce the desired functionalities.
thought.decision: To introduce the ketone and hydroxyl group, chose 1,2-addition of (1-ethoxyvinyl)lithium 29 to 30, expecting to form α-hydroxyketone 28 after hydrolysis.

outcome.observation: α-Hydroxyketone 28 was obtained in 69% yield after a one-pot hydrolysis of the enol ether.

evidence.text_span: Subsequent 1,2-addition of (1-ethoxyvinyl)- lithium 29 to 30 gave α-hydroxyketone 28 in 69% yield after a one-pot hydrolysis of the enol ether.
evidence.section: Scheme 2. Total Syntheses of (±)-Phaeocaulisin A and Cyclopropanol Ring-Opening Carbonylation to γ-Ketoesters

thought.background: Intermediate 28 has been synthesized. The next step is to form a spirocyclic lactone via an aldol reaction and subsequent lactonization.
thought.gap: How to achieve the desired aldol reaction and spontaneous lactonization.
thought.decision: To form the spirocyclic lactone, chose an aldol reaction of 28 with the lithium enolate derived from 27, expecting spontaneous lactonization to afford spirocyclic lactone 34.

outcome.observation: The aldol product underwent spontaneous lactonization to afford spirocyclic lactone 34 in 58% yield.

evidence.text_span: The subsequent aldol reaction of 28 with the lithium enolate derived from 27 occurred smoothly, but the initial aldol product underwent spontaneous lactonization to afford spirocyclic lactone 34 (CCDC 2292481) in 58% yield.
evidence.section: Scheme 2. Total Syntheses of (±)-Phaeocaulisin A and Cyclopropanol Ring-Opening Carbonylation to γ-Ketoesters

thought.background: Spirocyclic lactone 34 has been synthesized. The next step in the initial retrosynthetic plan was to form a cyclopropanol via an intramolecular Kulinkovich reaction.
thought.gap: How to achieve the intramolecular Kulinkovich cyclopropanol synthesis from 34 or its TMS ether 35.
thought.decision: To synthesize cyclopropanol 36, attempted various Kulinkovich cyclopropanol synthesis conditions on 34 or its TMS ether 35, expecting cyclopropanol formation.

outcome.observation: Various Kulinkovich cyclopropanol synthesis conditions failed to produce cyclopropanol 36 from 34 or its TMS ether 35.

evidence.text_span: Unfortunately, various Kulinkovich cyclopropanol synthesis conditions failed to produce cyclopropanol 36 from 34 or its TMS ether 35 despite the proximity of the lactone and the terminal olefin as indicated by the crystal structure of 34 (see the Supporting Information).
evidence.section: Scheme 2. Total Syntheses of (±)-Phaeocaulisin A and Cyclopropanol Ring-Opening Carbonylation to γ-Ketoesters

thought.background: The intramolecular Kulinkovich reaction failed. A new strategy is needed to access the cyclopropanol precursor.
thought.gap: How to synthesize a cyclopropanol that can be used in the subsequent ring-opening carbonylation.
thought.decision: To overcome the failure of the intramolecular Kulinkovich reaction, proposed an intermolecular Kulinkovich reaction to synthesize cyclopropanol 38 from 37, expecting to enable the subsequent cyclopropanol ring-opening carbonylation.

evidence.text_span: We then proposed an intermolecular Kulinkovich reaction18 to synthesize cyclopropanol 38 from 37 (Scheme 2B).
evidence.section: Scheme 2. Total Syntheses of (±)-Phaeocaulisin A and Cyclopropanol Ring-Opening Carbonylation to γ-Ketoesters

thought.background: The revised plan involves an intermolecular Kulinkovich reaction. First, the substrate for this reaction needs to be prepared.
thought.decision: To prepare 42, chose a 1,2-addition with 37 followed by one-pot TMS protection, expecting to form 42 in good yield.

outcome.observation: Compound 42 was prepared from 30 via a 1,2-addition with 37 followed by one-pot TMS protection in 88% yield.

evidence.text_span: In the revised approach (Scheme 2C), 42 was prepared from 30 via a 1,2-addition with 37 followed by one-pot TMS protection. Following Cha and co-workers’ protocol,18c a mixture (2:1) of cyclopropanols 43a and 43b was synthesized from 42 in 83% yield
evidence.section: Scheme 2. Total Syntheses of (±)-Phaeocaulisin A and Cyclopropanol Ring-Opening Carbonylation to γ-Ketoesters

thought.background: Intermediate 42 has been synthesized. The next step is the intermolecular Kulinkovich reaction to form the cyclopropanol.
thought.gap: How to synthesize cyclopropanols 43a and 43b from 42.
thought.decision: To synthesize cyclopropanols 43a and 43b, chose Cha and co-workers’ protocol using ClTi(O-iPr)3, c-C5H9MgCl, and MeOAc, expecting a mixture of diastereomers.

outcome.observation: A mixture (2:1) of cyclopropanols 43a and 43b was synthesized from 42 in 83% yield.

evidence.text_span: Following Cha and co-workers’ protocol,18c a mixture (2:1) of cyclopropanols 43a and 43b was synthesized from 42 in 83% yield
evidence.section: Scheme 2. Total Syntheses of (±)-Phaeocaulisin A and Cyclopropanol Ring-Opening Carbonylation to γ-Ketoesters

thought.background: Cyclopropanols 43a and 43b have been synthesized. The next key step is the palladium-catalyzed cyclopropanol ring-opening carbonylation.
thought.gap: How to achieve the ring-opening carbonylation to access the γ-ketoester, especially with an unprotected cyclopropanol.
thought.decision: To perform the ring-opening carbonylation, initially treated the mixture of 43a and 43b with an excess amount of Pd(OAc)2 and triethylamine in methanol under a carbon monoxide balloon, expecting to isolate γ-ketoester 45a as the major product.

outcome.observation: A mixture containing γ-ketoester 45a as the major product was isolated. This initial attempt used stoichiometric palladium.

evidence.text_span: which was then treated with an excess amount of Pd(OAc)2 together with triethylamine in methanol under a carbon monoxide balloon. We were delighted to isolate a mixture containing γ-ketoester 45a as the major product (see the Supporting Information).
evidence.section: Scheme 2. Total Syntheses of (±)-Phaeocaulisin A and Cyclopropanol Ring-Opening Carbonylation to γ-Ketoesters

thought.background: The stoichiometric palladium-catalyzed carbonylation worked, but a catalytic version is desired for efficiency.
thought.gap: How to make the cyclopropanol ring-opening carbonylation catalytic and improve the yield and selectivity.
thought.decision: To render the reaction catalytic, identified DDQ as an external oxidant, allowing the use of 10 mol% of Pd(OAc)2. Noted that acetal lactone 44 was the primary carbonylation product under catalytic conditions, so a subsequent one-pot alkaline workup with K2CO3 and KOH was needed to open the lactone and convert 44 to a separable mixture of γ-ketoesters 45a and 45b.

outcome.observation: Under catalytic conditions, acetal lactone 44 was the primary carbonylation product, which was then converted to a 2:1 separable mixture of γ-ketoesters 45a and 45b in total 67% yield after an alkaline workup.

evidence.text_span: To render this reaction catalytic, we identified DDQ as an external oxidant, which allows the use of 10 mol % of Pd(OAc)2 . Interestingly, under the catalytic conditions, acetal lactone 44 was identified as the primary carbonylation product. A subsequent one-pot alkaline workup with a mixture of K2CO3 and KOH was needed to open the lactone and convert 44 to a 2:1 separable mixture of γ-ketoesters 45a and 45b (the C7-epimer of 45a, not shown) in total 67% yield.
evidence.section: Scheme 2. Total Syntheses of (±)-Phaeocaulisin A and Cyclopropanol Ring-Opening Carbonylation to γ-Ketoesters

thought.background: The γ-ketoester 45a has been successfully synthesized. The isopropenyl group needs to be converted to a methyl ketone.
thought.gap: How to convert the isopropenyl group to the required methyl ketone.
thought.decision: To convert the isopropenyl group to a methyl ketone, chose ozonolysis, expecting to produce 46a.

outcome.observation: The isopropenyl group was converted to the required methyl ketone via ozonolysis, and 46a was produced in 78% yield.

evidence.text_span: After establishing the carbonylation method to prepare 45a, the isopropenyl group was converted to the required methyl ketone via ozonolysis and 46a was produced in 78% yield.
evidence.section: Scheme 2. Total Syntheses of (±)-Phaeocaulisin A and Cyclopropanol Ring-Opening Carbonylation to γ-Ketoesters

thought.background: Intermediate 46a has been synthesized. The next critical step is the intramolecular aldol cyclization to form the seven-membered ring.
thought.gap: How to achieve the desired aldol cyclization to form the seven-membered ring without undesired side reactions.
thought.decision: To close the seven-membered ring, treated 46a with LiHMDS in THF at -78 °C, expecting the desired intramolecular aldol reaction.

outcome.observation: Compound 48 with an unprecedented tetracyclic skeleton was obtained as the major product in 68% yield, resulting from undesired aldol cyclizations and lactone formation. The desired product 49 was only observed as a minor product.

evidence.text_span: When 46a was treated with LiHMDS in THF at −78 °C, to our surprise compound 48 (CCDC 2292484) with an unprecedented tetracyclic skeleton was obtained as the major product in 68% yield. The formation of 48 was proposed to start with one of the undesired aldol cyclizations between C15 and C8. The primary aldol product then reacted with the ester group to form tricyclic lactone 47. Upon further deprotonation at the α-position (C11) of the lactone, a second intramolecular aldol reaction occurred between C11 and C10 to produce 48. Additionally, the desired product 49 could be observed by crude NMR as the minor product.
evidence.section: Scheme 2. Total Syntheses of (±)-Phaeocaulisin A and Cyclopropanol Ring-Opening Carbonylation to γ-Ketoesters

thought.background: The initial aldol cyclization with LiHMDS led to an undesired tetracyclic product 48 as the major product. A more selective base is needed.
thought.gap: How to achieve the desired intramolecular aldol cyclization to form 49 as the major product.
thought.decision: To improve selectivity for 49, evaluated a list of bases and identified LDA and LiTMP as proper bases, expecting to favor deprotonation at the C9 position and lead to the desired product.

outcome.observation: LDA was identified as a proper base to produce desired product 49 as a major product in 58% yield on a scale-up, along with 48 as a minor product. LiTMP also showed good results on a small scale.

evidence.text_span: After evaluating a list of bases (Scheme 2E), LDA and LiTMP were identified as proper bases to produce desired product 49 (CCDC 2292485) as a major product in 54% and 63% NMR yield, respectively, on a small scale, together with 48 as a minor product. While slightly higher yield and selectivity were obtained with LiTMP at the exploration stage, the use of LDA worked better for scale up.
evidence.section: Scheme 2. Total Syntheses of (±)-Phaeocaulisin A and Cyclopropanol Ring-Opening Carbonylation to γ-Ketoesters

thought.background: Intermediate 49 has been successfully synthesized. The next step is to form the oxa bridge and γ-butyrolactone to complete the tetracyclic core.
thought.gap: How to remove the TMS group and trigger a spontaneous ketalization and lactonization process.
thought.decision: To form the oxa bridge and γ-butyrolactone, chose to remove the TMS group on the angular hydroxy group with fluoroboric acid to trigger a spontaneous ketalization and lactonization. Subsequently, subjected the resulting silyl fluoride intermediate to Fleming–Tamao oxidation with hydrogen peroxide and potassium fluoride, expecting to give 50.

outcome.observation: The TMS group was removed, triggering ketalization and lactonization. The resulting tetracyclic intermediate was subjected to Fleming–Tamao oxidation, giving 50 in 52% yield over two steps.

evidence.text_span: With 49 in hand, we then removed the TMS group on the angular hydroxy group with fluoroboric acid to trigger a spontaneous ketalization and lactonization process to form the oxa bridge and γ-butyrolactone in one step. Under the same reaction conditions, electrophilic cleavage of the Si−Ph bond occurred as well. The resulting tetracyclic intermediate with a silyl fluoride was next subjected to Fleming−Tamao oxidation with hydrogen peroxide and potassium fluoride to give 50 in 52% yield over two steps.
evidence.section: Scheme 2. Total Syntheses of (±)-Phaeocaulisin A and Cyclopropanol Ring-Opening Carbonylation to γ-Ketoesters

thought.background: Intermediate 50 has been synthesized. The next step is to introduce the exocyclic methylene moiety.
thought.gap: How to introduce the exocyclic methylene moiety efficiently.
thought.decision: To introduce the exocyclic methylene moiety, explored protocols using Eschenmoser’s salt, expecting successful methylenation.

outcome.observation: Protocols using Eschenmoser’s salt were unsuccessful.

evidence.text_span: To introduce the exocyclic methylene moiety, we first explored the protocols of using Eschenmoser’s salt22 and were unsuccessful.
evidence.section: Scheme 2. Total Syntheses of (±)-Phaeocaulisin A and Cyclopropanol Ring-Opening Carbonylation to γ-Ketoesters

thought.background: The attempt to introduce the exocyclic methylene moiety using Eschenmoser’s salt failed. An alternative method is needed.
thought.gap: How to successfully introduce the α-methylene group.
thought.decision: To introduce the α-methylene group, examined a one-pot α-carboxylation (NaH, (COOEt)2) and α-hydroxymethylation (K2CO3, (HCHO)n) followed by elimination, expecting to isolate 21.

outcome.observation: A one-pot α-carboxylation and α-hydroxymethylation followed by elimination successfully isolated 21 in 73% yield.

evidence.text_span: We then examined a one-pot α-carboxylation (NaH, (COOEt)2 ,) and α-hydroxymethylation (K2CO3 , (HCHO)n ) followed by elimination to install the α-methylene group23 and were able to isolate 21 in 73% yield.
evidence.section: Scheme 2. Total Syntheses of (±)-Phaeocaulisin A and Cyclopropanol Ring-Opening Carbonylation to γ-Ketoesters

thought.background: Intermediate 21, with the α-methylene γ-butyrolactone moiety, has been synthesized. The final step is to isomerize it to (±)-phaeocaulisin A.
thought.gap: How to achieve the alkene migration to form the desired endocyclic α,β-unsaturated γ-butyrolactone.
thought.decision: To isomerize 21 to (±)-phaeocaulisin A, chose ruthenium-catalyzed alkene migration, expecting high yield.

outcome.observation: Final ruthenium-catalyzed alkene migration of 21 successfully gave (±)-phaeocaulisin A in 90% yield.

evidence.text_span: To our delight, final ruthenium-catalyzed alkene migration13 of 21 successfully gave (±)-phaeocaulisin A in 90% yield.
evidence.section: Scheme 2. Total Syntheses of (±)-Phaeocaulisin A and Cyclopropanol Ring-Opening Carbonylation to γ-Ketoesters

Longest Linear Steps.interpretation: Total number of steps in the longest linear sequence.

Total Steps.interpretation: Total number of steps in the synthesis.

Failed Attempts Count.interpretation: Number of explicitly reported failed attempts.

validation.comparison_to_prior: The reported synthesis is 10 steps, compared to Procter's 17 LLS steps. The current synthesis provides (±)-phaeocaulisin A, while Procter's synthesis yielded the (-)-enantiomer. The current work also identified intermediate 21 as significantly more potent against cancer cell lines than phaeocaulisin A itself, which was not explored in prior syntheses.
validation.significance: This work presents a concise 10-step total synthesis of (±)-phaeocaulisin A, enabled by a novel palladium-catalyzed cyclopropanol ring-opening carbonylation, chemo- and stereoselective aldol cyclization, and cascade ketalization-lactonization. It expands the utility of palladium-catalyzed cyclopropanol ring-opening chemistry and identifies a promising lead compound (21) with enhanced anticancer proliferation activity against triple-negative or HER2+ breast cancer cell lines, highlighting the biological importance of the α-methylene γ-butyrolactone moiety.

source.title: Ten-Step Total Synthesis of (±)-Phaeocaulisin A Enabled by Cyclopropanol Ring-Opening Carbonylation.
source.authors: Liu C, Zhang M, Zeng L, Wan Y, Dai M.
